candidates.reasoning: The landscape is highly characteristic of the Great Basin or Intermountain West, featuring sagebrush-dominated steppe, arid rolling hills, and wide-open vistas. This specific combination of vegetation and terrain is very common in Nevada and eastern Oregon/Washington.
The interior plateaus of British Columbia or Alberta share very similar semi-arid, rolling grassland and scrubland characteristics with the US Intermountain West.
The vast, treeless steppe and rolling hills are geographically similar, though the road infrastructure and utility pole style are less typical for rural Mongolia compared to North America.
The high plateaus (Meseta) of central Spain can exhibit similar arid, rolling terrain and scrubland, though the specific vegetation looks more North American.

evidence: Wide, paved two-lane road with minimal shoulder
Arid climate with sparse, low-lying shrubs
Utility poles following a linear road pattern in a remote area

candidates.reasoning: The landscape is dominated by sagebrush (Artemisia tridentata) and bunchgrasses, which are characteristic of the Great Basin and Intermountain West regions. This specific combination of arid shrub-steppe vegetation and rolling hills is highly typical of Nevada or Utah.

Similar shrub-steppe ecosystems exist in the interior plateau of British Columbia and Alberta. The vegetation is botanically similar to the US Great Basin, though the topography here is more consistent with the American West.

The road construction, asphalt quality, and utility pole design are highly consistent with rural North American standards. The lack of road markings on a secondary rural road is common in the Western US.
Infrastructure standards in rural Canada are nearly identical to the USA, including the style of utility poles and road surfacing seen here.
The landscape and basic paved road without markings are consistent with Mongolian rural infrastructure, though the utility pole style is more typical of North America.

The utility pole is a simple wooden pole with a single cross-arm, which is extremely common in rural North American landscapes. The style of the small, weathered wooden outbuilding/shed on the left is characteristic of rural American West architecture.
Wooden utility poles of this design are also standard across rural Canada. The overall infrastructure and building style are nearly identical to the USA, making it a strong regional candidate.

evidence: wooden utility pole with single cross-arm
